title: A q-factor detection method
abstract: A method of measuring a Q-factor in a wireless power transmitter includes charging a capacitor in a LC tank circuit that includes a transmission coil to a voltage; starting a Q-factor determining by coupling the LC tank circuit to ground to form a free-oscillating circuit; monitoring the voltage across the capacitor as a function of time as the LC tank circuit oscillates; and determining the resonant frequency and the Q-factor from monitoring the voltage.
claims:
Claims Claims What is claimed is: 1. A wireless power transmitter, comprising: a transmit coil; a capacitor coupled in series with the transmit coil to form a resonant circuit; a bridge circuit coupled to the resonant circuit; a control circuit coupled to control the bridge circuit to provide voltages across the resonant circuit; a charging circuit coupled to charge the capacitor with a charging voltage and coupled to be controlled by the control circuit; and a detection circuit coupled to receive a voltage across the capacitor and provide data related to the voltage to the control circuit while the resonant circuit. 2. The transmitter of claim 1 , wherein the control circuit includes a processor that executes instructions to: charge the capacitor; once the capacitor is charged, set the transmit coil and the series capacitor into a free oscillation; receive the data from the detection circuit; and determine a Q-factor from the data. 3. The transmitter of claim 2, wherein the charging circuit includes: a resistor coupled to a node between the transmit coil and the capacitor; a charging transistor coupled between a voltage output to provide the charging voltage of the control circuit and the resistor. 4. The transmitter of claim 3, wherein the charging voltage does not activate a receiver placed proximate to the transmission coil. 5. The transmitter 3, wherein the bridge circuit comprises: a first transistor coupled between a voltage rail and the transmit coil opposite the capacitor; 

 a second transistor coupled between the voltage rail and the capacitor opposite the transmit coil; a third transistor coupled between the transmit coil opposite the capacitor and ground; and a fourth transistor coupled between the capacitor opposite the transmit coil and ground. 6. The transmitter of claim 5, wherein the instructions to charge the capacitor include instructions to turn the first transistor, the second transistor, and the third transistor to off; turn the fourth transistor to on; and turn the charging transistor to on; 7. The transmitter of claim 6, wherein instructions to set the transmit coil and the series capacitor into free oscillation includes instructions to turn the charging transistor to off; measure an initial voltage across the capacitor; and turn the third transistor on. 8. The transmitter of claim 2, wherein the data represents a decaying sinusoidal of the free oscillation and wherein the control circuit executes instructions to determine a decay of an envelope of the data; receive a resonant frequency from the detection circuit; determine a voltage of the envelope at a time T; and determine the Q factor from the voltage of the envelope. 9. The transmitter of claim 2, wherein the data represents a decaying sinusoidal of the free oscillation and wherein the control circuit executes instructions to count a number of oscillations until a peak envelope voltage reaches Â½ of an initial voltage; determine the Q-factor from the number. 10. A method of measuring a Q-factor in a wireless power transmitter, comprising: 

 charging a capacitor in a LC tank circuit that includes a transmission coil to a voltage; starting a Q-factor determining by coupling the LC tank circuit to ground to form a free-oscillating circuit; monitoring the voltage across the capacitor as a function of time as the LC tank circuit oscillates; and determining the resonant frequency and the Q-factor from monitoring the voltage. 1 1. The method of claim 10, wherein charging a capacitor in the LC tank circuit includes coupling a first end of the capacitor to ground; applying a voltage through a resistance to a second end opposite the first end of the capacitor, wherein the transmit coil is coupled to the second end; and removing the capacitor voltage when the capacitor is considered to be charged. 12. The method of claim 1 1, wherein the voltage does not activate a receiver proximate to the transmit coil. 13. The method of claim 1 1 , wherein starting the Q-factor determ ination includes determining an initial voltage across the capacitor after charging; coupling the transmit coil to ground to form the free resonating circuit. 14. The method of claim 13, wherein monitoring the voltage includes measuring the capacitor voltage as a function of time, the capacitor voltage being a decaying sinusoidal signal. 15. The method of claim 14, wherein determining the resonant frequency and the Q-factor includes determining the resonant frequency from the sinusoidal portion of the decaying sinusoidal signal; and determining the Q-factor from the decaying portion of the decaying sinusoidal signal. 16. The method of claim 10, further including comparing the Q-factor with a threshold value; and 

 indicating the presence of a foreign object if the Q-factor is less than the threshold value. 17. A wireless power transmitter, comprising: means for charging a capacitor in a resonant circuit that includes a transmit coil and the capacitor; means for placing the resonant circuit in a free oscillation; means for sampling a voltage across the capacitor as a function of time; means for determining a resonant frequency of the resonant circuit from the sampling; and means for determining a Q-factor from the sampling. 18. The wireless power transmitter of claim 18, further including means for determining presence of a foreign object from the Q-factor.
